paragraphs.text:
Interpretation and rationale:
- Sulfur donors: Deprotonated thiolates (e.g., cysteine, glutathione) yield exceptionally large log K values (K1 ≈ 19–21; β2 ≈ 38–42), far exceeding N- and O-donor examples under comparable conditions. Even a neutral thione S donor (thiourea) is stronger than typical N donors.
- Nitrogen donors: Moderate binding (en K1 = 8.8; Im K1 = 5.3; β2 ≤ ~14), well below thiolate complexes by >10 orders of magnitude for K1.
- Oxygen donors: Generally weak (acetate K1 = 1.5; β2 ≈ 2.7). Citrate shows larger cumulative stability (β1 ≈ 14) due to multidentate chelation, but on a per-donor/stepwise basis still trails far behind thiolates.

claims.text: Deprotonated thiolates (e.g., cysteine, glutathione) yield exceptionally large log K values
far exceeding N- and O-donor examples under comparable conditions.
Even a neutral thione S donor (thiourea) is stronger than typical N donors.
well below thiolate complexes by >10 orders of magnitude for K1.
but on a per-donor/stepwise basis still trails far behind thiolates.

Chelate effect: higher cumulative stability arises from multidentate ligands; this is general coordination chemistry, not shown in database rows here.
Per-donor/stepwise constants for O-donor ligands are typically lower than for thiolates with Hg(II); general trend, not numerically shown here.

paragraphs.text: Conclusion: The order S ≫ N > O is strongly supported by these data. This aligns with HSAB expectations for Hg(II) as a soft/borderline acid that forms its most stable complexes with soft thiolate sulfur donors. The differences are robust across media (nitrate/perchlorate) and ionic strengths used here and span many orders of magnitude in stability.

forms its most stable complexes with soft thiolate sulfur donors
The differences are robust across media (nitrate/perchlorate) and ionic strengths used here
span many orders of magnitude in stability.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: HSAB theory (soft acids prefer soft bases) is general chemistry knowledge; SRD-46 tool outputs here do not include HSAB classifications.
Hg(II) being soft/borderline is standard HSAB classification; this property is not provided in the tool results shown.
That Hg(II) forms its most stable complexes with soft thiolate S donors is established HSAB behavior; not directly tabulated in the tool outputs.
Electrolyte media (nitrate/perchlorate) are not shown; only ionic strength ranges appear. Thus 'across media' cannot be verified here.
No stability constant values (logK, β) are displayed in the tool results; cannot show that differences span many orders of magnitude.
